title: Wealth, Whiteness, and the Matrix of Privilege: The View from the Country Club By Jessica Holden Sherwood Lexington Books. 2010. 188 pages. $60.00 cloth
abstract: Through 45 in-depth interviews with elite country club members located in the northeastern United States, Jessica Holden Sherwood provides an interesting glimpse into the privileged and enclosed world of the upper class and elite White Anglo Saxon Protestants (WASPs). Contrary to the popular notion that private country clubs are social institutions dedicated to the leisure activities of the upper and elite classes, Sherwood exposes them as important locations where social and cultural capital and social networks are created and maintained and made increasingly unavailable to outsiders. Sherwood does a sophisticated job of peeling back the layers on the “accounts” (“things people say to justify or excuse something that might appear questionable”[xiv]) that her interviewees strategically construct when questioned about topics related to the exclusivity of elite clubs and diversity along the lines of race, ethnicity and gender. A central and recurring theme in the book is the issue of exclusivity, which is thoroughly explored in Chapters 1 and 2. The semiformal and highly subjective interview process for membership provides insight into how these clubs have managed to remain exclusively white and elite. “Homophily – mixing with people like yourself,”(16) underlies clubs' exclusionary practices, as it is a necessary practice to maintain physical and social distance between club members and outsiders.
